modal.payAttention: When families may want to pay closer attention
modal.disclaimer: Friendly info only — not a substitute for your nurse line, doctor, or 911.

rsv.headline: RSV (respiratory syncytial virus)
rsv.whatItIs: A very common virus that loves little airways. Most kids meet it more than once — it usually looks like a cold, but babies and young toddlers can work harder to breathe.
commonSigns: Stuffy or runny nose, cough, sometimes a fever
Faster breathing, belly pulling in under the ribs, or noisy breathing in small babies
More tired than usual or tricky feeds / fewer wet diapers
prevention: Wash hands like a playground ritual — especially before snacks
Keep sick family members cuddled a little apart when you can
Clean high-touch toys and surfaces on the extra-gunky days
Rest, fluids, and patience — most kiddos bounce back with time
payAttention: Breathing looks hard, lips or face look blue-ish, or they’re pulling in at the neck or ribs
So sleepy it’s tough to wake, or not drinking / very few wet diapers
Under 3 months with a fever, or your gut says “this isn’t our usual cold”
When in doubt, your nurse line or clinic is the right call — that’s what they’re for

flu.headline: Influenza (flu)
flu.whatItIs: A seasonal virus that can wallop the whole house at once. It often hits harder and faster than a gentle cold — but plenty of families ride it out with rest and fluids.
commonSigns: Sudden fever, chills, body aches, and “I just want the couch” energy
Sore throat, cough, headache, sometimes tummy upset in kids
Everyone in the house sick within a few days — classic flu timing
prevention: Annual flu vaccine for the family (ask your care team what’s right for you)
Hand washing, tissues, and elbow-coughs — boring but powerful
If someone’s sick, cozy movie days beat big gatherings
Sleep and snacks — simple care helps immune systems do their job
payAttention: Breathing trouble, chest pain, confusion, or not waking normally
Not keeping fluids down, or signs of dehydration
Fever that worries you in a young infant, or symptoms that snap back worse
Your local nurse line or doctor can help you decide “home vs. check-in”

covid.headline: COVID-19
covid.whatItIs: A respiratory virus that’s still part of the seasonal mix. In kids it often looks like a cold or stomach bug — testing and public guidance change over time, so local health sites stay handy.
commonSigns: Runny nose, cough, sore throat, fever — the usual “something’s going around” bundle
Sometimes headache, tiredness, or tummy trouble instead of classic cold signs
Loss of smell/taste can happen but isn’t the headline in every child
prevention: Stay home when sick when you can — kindness to daycare buddies included
Hand hygiene, fresh air, and up-to-date vaccines per your care team’s advice
Masks in crowded indoor spaces when community levels are up — family choice + local guidance
Open windows on nice days — airflow is underrated
payAttention: Breathing that doesn’t look comfortable, blue lips, or severe chest pain
Confusion, dehydration, or fever in a baby that worries you
Follow current BC / Canada guidance for testing and return-to-school rules
Call 911 for emergencies; otherwise your clinic or nurse line is your compass

air_quality.headline: Air quality
air_quality.whatItIs: A snapshot of how comfy outside air is for sensitive lungs — smoke, dust, and pollution can all nudge the dial. LittleBuggy turns that into plain language, not scary numbers.
commonSigns: Kids may cough more, rub eyes, or get tired faster on “rough air” days
Children with asthma or sensitive airways might need their usual plan a bit sooner
Sometimes you just notice haze or a campfire smell — that’s a cue
prevention: Choose gentler outdoor windows when the air looks hazy or apps flag it
Indoor play, air purifiers if you have them, and closed windows on smoky days
Hydration and slower pace — low-key park hangs beat sprinting in smog
payAttention: Coughing that won’t settle, wheezing, or working hard to breathe
Follow local air-quality advisories for schools and sports
When in doubt, shorten outside time and check in with your care team

outdoor.headline: Outdoor feel
outdoor.whatItIs: A plain-language blend of air quality and weather — one line about whether the backyard or the living-room fort is the cozier pick today.
commonSigns: Not a diagnosis — just “how it might feel” for sniffly or sensitive kids
Rough days might mean shorter outside time; nice days might mean scooter time
prevention: Pack layers and water; check the air-quality card on smoky or hazy days
Save big park adventures for clearer-air windows when you can
payAttention: Trust your eyes on smoke, storms, or kids who seem wiped out fast
Medical questions still go to your nurse line or doctor — we’re the soft nudge, not the chart

cough_patterns.headline: Coughs & fevers (general)
cough_patterns.whatItIs: Lots of bugs share the same soundtrack — cough plus fever is a combo families talk about a lot. Patterns and how your child acts between spikes matter more than any single number.
commonSigns: Cough dry or wet, worse at night, sometimes with fever that comes and goes
Energy bouncing back between fevers often points toward “working through it”
Stuffy nose, sore throat, or tummy upset riding along
prevention: Fluids, rest, humidifier if it helps your crew sleep
Hand washing and not sharing cups — classic sibling diplomacy
Keep vaccinations and check-ups current so you’ve got a baseline
payAttention: Breathing that looks labored, blue lips, or not drinking
Lethargy that’s not like them, or fever in a young infant
Cough that keeps getting worse instead of slowly easing

how_it_works.headline: Where our data comes from
how_it_works.whatItIs: LittleBuggy gathers a few trusted public signals and wraps them in plain, parent-friendly language. Here’s how that comes together — and what it can (and can’t) do for your family.
how_it_works.s1Intro: We combine automated feeds with gentle editorial framing. When something isn’t a live number from a feed, we try to say so on the page.
s1Items: Public health signals — for Metro Vancouver we use population-level respiratory trend summaries from Canada’s public Health Infobase (BC wastewater-style signals). That’s a bird’s-eye view of what’s circulating, not a lab test or diagnosis for any one child. BCCDC still publishes separate clinical summaries for professionals.
Air quality — AQHI-style readings from Environment and Climate Change Canada’s open MSC GeoMet data for the region.
Weather and outdoor feel — current conditions from Open-Meteo (Vancouver area), then translated into simple “how today might feel outside” wording.
Editorial, parent-friendly summaries — copy on the site is written to help you scan quickly and feel oriented. It’s not a substitute for reading official guidance when you need detail.
s2Items: Air quality — refreshed frequently; in normal operation, typically every day when our backend environment job runs.
Weather — refreshed frequently, on the same rhythm as the daily environment update.
Respiratory trend summaries — updated on a slower cadence (about weekly), because the underlying public data isn’t meant to change minute-by-minute.
Editorial content and comfort copy — revised when we ship updates; the timestamp on the live snapshot tells you when the last automated bundle was saved.
s3Items: Some editorial visuals and story cards are simplified — they add warmth and season context, not street-level or neighbourhood-precise health mapping.
Not every card is a live epidemiology feed. Sections labeled as editorial or illustrative are there to reflect typical parent chatter and season patterns, not to report a real-time case count.
We’d rather be clear than flashy: if a fetch fails or data is old, we show friendly stand-ins and explain what you’re looking at.
how_it_works.s4Lead: LittleBuggy is a helper for everyday context — quick, warm, and multilingual — but it is not medical care.
s4Items: It isn’t a diagnosis tool and it doesn’t examine your child.
It doesn’t replace your doctor, nurse line, or emergency services. If something feels urgent, reach out to the people trained for that.
For medical decisions, testing, and outbreak details, rely on official BC and Canada health information.

app.awareness: LittleBuggy feels like a chat with a thoughtful neighbour — not a replacement for your nurse line or doctor, but a faster way to read local health chatter ✨
app.footer: {brand} is built for multilingual families in Canada — friendly local health context in a language you can scan quickly. Not medical advice; your care team knows your child best.
app.langPanelHint: Seven languages below — Korean, Japanese, Hindi, Filipino, and Vietnamese use English copy until their locale files ship.
app.langRoadmap: We’re filling in Korean, Japanese, Hindi, Filipino, and Vietnamese translations over time — the layout stays the same.

waitTimes.title: Emergency Wait Times
waitTimes.lead: Estimated waits at selected Lower Mainland ERs. Updates when the source posts new numbers.
waitTimes.errorGeneric: We couldn’t load wait times just now. You can retry in a moment.
waitTimes.errorNetwork: We couldn’t reach the server. Check your connection and try again.
waitTimes.nearTimestampsNote: Numbers may stay the same if the hospital source has not published a new update yet.
waitTimes.refreshHonestyNote: Wait times may not change on every refresh if the source has not posted a newer update — a fresh check can still show the same minutes.
waitTimes.nonePosted: No posted wait times for the hospitals we track in the latest pull—try refreshing in a moment.
waitTimes.disclaimer: Wait times are estimates only and may change quickly. Patients are seen based on urgency, not arrival order. Call 911 for life-threatening emergencies.
waitTimes.sourceNote: Data is fetched live from the public BC emergency wait times service (edwaittimes.ca) and shown here for convenience.

upccWaitTimes.title: Urgent & Primary Care (UPCC) Wait Times
upccWaitTimes.lead: Same-day urgent care (not emergencies). Call 911 for life-threatening problems.
upccWaitTimes.disclaimer: UPCC visits are for urgent (not routine) issues when you can’t see your usual clinic. Waits are estimates; you’ll be seen based on clinical need. Call 911 for life-threatening emergencies.
upccWaitTimes.sourceNote: Pulled from the same public BC wait-times service as the ER list (edwaittimes.ca), filtered to UPCC locations.
upccWaitTimes.legacyNoUpcc: When we use the backup ER page, UPCC waits aren’t listed here. Try again in a few minutes.
upccWaitTimes.emptyList: No posted waits for these centres in the latest pull—try again soon.

readGuide.title: How to read this week’s snapshot
readGuide.deck: A quick orientation — what the cards mean, and what they are not.
readGuide.step1: Start with the headline, then scan the respiratory row (RSV / flu / COVID-style signals), then air and weather when they’re shown.
readGuide.step2: Treat this as a regional, population-level sketch from public sources — useful context, not a test result or diagnosis for your child.
readGuide.step3: Not medical advice. If something feels wrong, use your nurse line, doctor, or emergency services.

tc1.text: Tiny coughs, noisy breathing, and “should we worry?” — especially with the littlest kids.

tc3.title: Coughs that stick around

rsv.title: RSV
rsv.symptoms: Wheezy breathing, coughs, and tricky feeds for babies.

flu.title: Flu
flu.symptoms: Bam — fever, achy body, zero energy.

covid.title: COVID
covid.symptoms: Cough, fever, sore throat — or sometimes just an off tummy day.

cold.title: Common Cold
cold.symptoms: Stuffy snoot, sneezes, and a mild cough.

stomach.title: Stomach Bug
stomach.symptoms: Tummy rumbles, upset hugs, and urgent bathroom trips.

compare.title: Symptoms families ask about
compare.deck: Gentle patterns when a seasonal bug is going around — not a diagnosis.

runny.tip: Thicker mucus plus feeling blah, sore throat, or fever often tags along with a virus that’s doing the rounds.

fever.tip: Pretty common when a bug is circulating — watch how your child drinks, rests, and bounces back between spikes.

sneeze.tip: Sneezes show up with lots of bugs; if they’re miserable in other ways too, think whole-picture care and fluids.

night.tip: Lying down can make mucus pool or tickle more — humidifier, fluids, and your nurse line if breathing looks hard.

energy.tip: Viruses often bring “couch magnet” days. If they’re hard to wake or not drinking, get help sooner.

noticing.title: What parents are noticing
noticing.deck: Little echoes from this week — the things grown-ups mention at swing sets and soccer sidelines.
noticing.n4: More talk about scratchy throats after outside play

asking.title: Questions parents are asking
asking.deck: Tap for a warm, plain-language nudge — your doctor or nurse still knows your kid best.

q1.q: How do I know if it’s “just a cold”?
q1.a: Colds usually bring a bundle: tired kiddo, sore throat, runny nose, sometimes fever. How fast it came on and what else is going around in daycare or school matters too. When you’re unsure, your nurse line or doctor is the best shortcut.

q2.q: Why is my child coughing more at night?
q2.a: Gravity! Mucus can pool or tickle more when they lie down. Dry bedroom air or irritated airways can pile on. Humidifier + fluids help lots of homes sleep better — reach out if breathing ever looks scary or sleep is a mess night after night.

q3.q: Why did the fever come back?
q3.a: Sometimes a second bug hops in before the first story is over, or their body is still sorting things out. Sips, snuggles, and watching how they act between spikes is the parent playbook. If they seem really off or you’re uneasy, your team is happy to hear from you.

q4.q: When is a cough too long?
q4.a: A drifty cough can hang around even after energy bounces back — that’s pretty normal. Trust your gut if breathing is tough, lips look blue, they’re not drinking, or the cough keeps ramping up instead of slowly fading. A quick call always beats worrying alone.

symptom.title: What are you seeing at home?
symptom.deck: Tap what fits — we’ll share what other local families have been mentioning in the same boat.
symptom.hint: Tap a bubble (or a few!) to explore — it’s just for curiosity and “same here” moments.

cough.label: A cough that won’t quit
cough.tip: Sound familiar? Many local families say coughs stick around a week or two while their kid perks up — ping someone if breathing sounds rough or sleep is a struggle.

fever.label: Warm forehead moments
fever.tip: Fevers that pop up, vanish, then say hi again are a hot playground topic. Fluids, rest, and how they’re acting between spikes are your best friends.

sneeze.label: Lots of sneezing
sneeze.tip: Sneezing shows up in many colds and flus — pair it with how they’re eating, sleeping, and acting overall.

stomach.label: Tummy troubles
stomach.tip: Yucky tummies love to visit every sibling in the house. Tiny sips and plain foods usually step in once the worst wave passes.

nose.label: Runny or stuffy nose
nose.tip: Daycare season’s background soundtrack! Thicker mucus plus feeling crummy often tracks with whatever virus is visiting that week.

seek.title: When to seek more help
seek.lead: Trust your gut. Call 911 for emergencies; otherwise nurse line or clinic.
lines: Hard breathing, blue lips, or loud wheezing
Very dry or hardly peeing, or hard to wake
Baby under 3 months with fever, or fever over 3 days
Nonstop vomiting, blood in stool, or severe belly pain
Confusion, stiff neck, or rash that doesn’t fade when pressed
